cards.definition: Non-living parts of an ecosystem that shape its environment, such as temperature, light, and water.
The use of energy (ATP) to move substances against the concentration gradient (low → high).
Tiny molecules that act as building blocks for proteins.
Molecules within DNA that code for proteins; arranged in complementary pairs: A with T, C with G.
Living organisms that shape an ecosystem, including plants, animals, fungi, and microbes.
The movement of carbon through air, water, soil, plants, and animals in ecosystems.
Packages of DNA that contain thousands of genes; humans have 46 chromosomes in 23 pairs.
Variation in traits that can have any value within a range (e.g., height, weight).
The carrier of genetic information, found in the nucleus of cells; double-stranded helix.
Variation in traits that fit into distinct categories (e.g., blood type, flower color).
Passive movement of particles from high to low concentration.
Adenine (A), Thymine (T), Cytosine (C), Guanine (G) – the building blocks of DNA.
A community of living organisms plus the non-living environment interacting together.
A section of DNA that contains instructions for making a protein or part of a protein.
The process where information in a gene is used to create a functional product, usually a protein.
The study of how DNA is passed from parents to offspring and how traits are inherited.
The complete set of genetic material (all genes) present in a cell or organism.
The genetic makeup of an organism; the set of genes it carries.
Chemical messengers produced by glands that regulate body processes by traveling through the blood.
The basic building blocks of DNA and RNA, consisting of a nitrogenous base, a sugar, and a phosphate group.
The control center of a cell where DNA is stored.
Passive movement of water through a semi-permeable membrane from low to high solute concentration.
The observable characteristics or traits of an organism, influenced by genes and environment.
Large molecules made of amino acid chains that build structures and carry out cellular tasks.
Ribonucleic acid, similar to DNA but single-stranded and contains Uracil (U) instead of Thymine (T).
Special proteins that attach to DNA to turn genes on or off by starting gene expression.
Differences in traits among individuals of a species.
Plant tissue that transports water and minerals from roots upwards.
Plant tissue that transports sugars from leaves to other parts of the plant.
Blood vessels that carry blood away from the heart, usually oxygen-rich.
Tiny blood vessels where exchange of oxygen, nutrients, and waste occurs between blood and cells.
Blood vessels that carry blood back to the heart, usually oxygen-poor.
The liquid part of blood that carries nutrients, hormones, water, and waste.
Blood cells that carry oxygen to body cells.
Blood cells that fight infections and pathogens.
Blood components that help with clotting to stop bleeding.
The process by which plants use light energy to make glucose from carbon dioxide and water.
Pores on the surface of leaves that allow gas exchange (CO₂ in, O₂ out).
Cells that surround stomata and control their opening and closing.
Māori lunar calendar based on natural signs used to guide planting, harvesting, and fishing.
Natural environmental signs used by Māori to monitor seasons and ecosystem health.
A guardian or protector, often referring to people or beings who care for the environment.
Something treasured or valued, including possessions, resources, ideas, or customs.
